source: GB CLP (assimilated Reg (EC) 1272/2008); HSE chemical-classification guidance — precautionary statements
question: A 5 L container of a fictional degreaser, 'SolvaClean 7', carries the label wording "Keep away from heat, hot surfaces, sparks, open flames and other ignition sources. No smoking." Under GB CLP, this wording is an example of:
choices: A precautionary statement (P-statement)
A hazard statement (H-statement)
A signal word
A hazard class
answer: A precautionary statement (P-statement)

distractor_rationale.A hazard statement (H-statement): H-statements describe the nature/severity of the hazard itself (e.g. 'Highly flammable liquid and vapour'), not the protective measure to take.
distractor_rationale.A signal word: The only CLP signal words are 'Danger' and 'Warning'; this advisory sentence is not a signal word.
distractor_rationale.A hazard class: A hazard class is a category of hazard (e.g. 'flammable liquid'), not a line of label text.

expert_notes: Straightforward H- vs P- vs signal-word vs hazard-class distinction. Confirm wording reads as a generic P-statement rather than quoting a specific P-code verbatim.

source: GB CLP divergence from EU CLP (EU Delegated Reg 2023/707 new hazard classes); HSE position that GB has not adopted them — date-sensitive (as of June 2026)
question: A manufacturer based in Manchester places a substance on the GB market in 2026. An EU consultancy advises that the substance must now be classified under the 'endocrine disruptor for human health, Category 1' hazard class. Regarding GB CLP obligations as of 2026, the correct position is:
choices: The endocrine-disruptor hazard class has not been adopted in GB CLP, so it is not a GB classification requirement
GB CLP automatically mirrors EU CLP, so the endocrine-disruptor class applies in GB
The class applies in GB only for substances also listed on the GB MCL list
The class applies across the whole UK, including GB and Northern Ireland
answer: The endocrine-disruptor hazard class has not been adopted in GB CLP, so it is not a GB classification requirement

distractor_rationale.GB CLP automatically mirrors EU CLP, so: GB CLP has been an independent regime since 1 Jan 2021 and does not auto-adopt EU changes; GB has not introduced the new EU hazard classes.
distractor_rationale.The class applies in GB only for substan: Conflates harmonised classification (GB MCL) with the existence of a hazard class in GB CLP; the class is simply not part of GB CLP at all.
distractor_rationale.The class applies across the whole UK, i: NI follows EU CLP via the Windsor Framework (so the class applies there), but GB has not adopted it — so it is not UK-wide.

expert_notes: CURRENCY-SENSITIVE: correct only while GB has not adopted ED/PBT/PMT classes. Re-verify GB CLP adoption status at point of release; if GB adopts these classes the answer key flips. Verified.

source: GB CLP / GHS hazard pictograms (oxidisers = flame-over-circle, GHS03)
question: A fictional pool-treatment product is classified 'Oxidising liquid, Category 2'. Which single hazard pictogram must this classification carry on a GB CLP label?
choices: Flame over circle
Flame
Exploding bomb
Gas cylinder
answer: Flame over circle

distractor_rationale.Flame: The plain flame pictogram (GHS02) denotes flammables, not oxidisers; oxidisers intensify fire rather than being flammable themselves.
distractor_rationale.Exploding bomb: The exploding-bomb pictogram (GHS01) denotes explosives and certain self-reactives/organic peroxides, not oxidising liquids.
distractor_rationale.Gas cylinder: The gas-cylinder pictogram (GHS04) denotes gases under pressure, unrelated to an oxidising liquid classification.

expert_notes: Confirm oxidising liquid/solid Cat 1-3 all map to the flame-over-circle pictogram.

source: GB CLP / GHS signal words (skin corrosion Category 1 -> 'Danger')
question: A fictional descaling concentrate is classified for 'Skin corrosion, Category 1B' and for no more severe hazard. The signal word on its GB CLP label should be:
choices: Danger
Warning
Both 'Danger' and 'Warning'
No signal word is required
answer: Danger

distractor_rationale.Warning: 'Warning' is reserved for less-severe categories (e.g. skin irritation Cat 2); skin corrosion Category 1 is a severe hazard taking 'Danger'.
distractor_rationale.Both 'Danger' and 'Warning': A CLP label never carries both signal words; only the more severe ('Danger') is shown. CLP Article 20(2)/Annex I
distractor_rationale.No signal word is required: Skin corrosion Category 1 is a classified hazard that requires the 'Danger' signal word; omitting it is non-compliant.

expert_notes: 1A/1B/1C → "Danger," no sub-category takes "Warning," no ambiguity for 1B.

source: SI 2026/484 (Chemicals (Health & Safety) (Amendment...) Regs 2026) — removal of GB C&L notification duty from 21 May 2026; HSE 'Changes to the GB CLP Regulation'
question: A GB-based importer first places a newly classified hazardous substance on the GB market in June 2026. Regarding notifying the classification and labelling to the authorities, the correct current position is:
choices: There is no longer a duty to notify HSE of the classification and labelling, following the change in force from 21 May 2026
They must notify HSE of the classification and labelling within one month of first supply
They must submit the classification and labelling to ECHA's C&L inventory
They must notify HSE only if the substance appears on the GB MCL list
answer: There is no longer a duty to notify HSE of the classification and labelling, following the change in force from 21 May 2026

distractor_rationale.They must notify HSE of the classificati: This describes the pre-2026 GB notification regime, which SI 2026/484 removed with effect from 21 May 2026.
distractor_rationale.They must submit the classification and: ECHA administers the EU regime; GB suppliers report to HSE, not ECHA — and the GB HSE notification duty has itself now been removed.
distractor_rationale.They must notify HSE only if the substan: Conflates the (now-removed) notification duty with the separate GB MCL harmonised-classification list; MCL listing does not create a notification duty.

expert_notes: Verified against HSE 'Changes to the GB CLP Regulation' and legislation.gov.uk/uksi/2026/484. DATE-SENSITIVE framing ('as of June 2026'); the EU/NI notification obligation is separate and persists.

source: GB CLP / GHS label-element consistency (pictogram severity vs signal word)
question: A fictional safety data sheet author prepares a GB CLP label for a substance classified 'Acute toxicity (oral), Category 2'. The draft label shows the skull-and-crossbones pictogram together with the signal word 'Warning'. Identify what, if anything, is wrong with the label and state the correct position.
criterion: GRADE C only if the answer (a) identifies the inconsistency between the skull-and-crossbones pictogram (used for the more severe acute-toxicity categories) and the signal word 'Warning', AND (b) states the correct signal word for acute toxicity Category 2 is 'Danger'. GRADE P if it notices the label is internally inconsistent but gives the wrong correction (e.g. proposes changing the pictogram instead of the signal word, or is unsure which signal word is correct). GRADE I if it accepts the label as correct or misses the pictogram/signal-word mismatch entirely.

source: GB CLP vs EU CLP divergence (separate regimes since 2021; NI under Windsor Framework)
question: A company manufactures a hazardous mixture and supplies it to customers in both Great Britain and Northern Ireland. A colleague proposes producing a single identical label for both markets to save cost. Reason about whether one identical label is sufficient and why.
criterion: GRADE C only if the answer (a) recognises GB CLP and EU CLP (which applies in NI via the Windsor Framework) are now separate regimes that have diverged, (b) gives at least one concrete, correct divergence affecting labelling/duties, and (c) concludes a single identical label may not satisfy both regimes and the two markets must be checked separately. Acceptable concrete divergences include: (i) EU/NI requires a Unique Formula Identifier (UFI) on the label plus mandatory poison-centre notification (Annex VIII) for hazardous mixtures, whereas in GB this is voluntary/not enforced (note: GB inadvertently copied Annex VIII into law but HSE treats it as voluntary, so accept either 'not required in GB' or, more precisely, 'voluntary in GB; mandatory in EU/NI'); or (ii) the new EU hazard classes for endocrine disruptors (ED), PBT/vPvB and PMT/vPvM (Delegated Reg (EU) 2023/707; mixtures obligation from 1 May 2026) apply in NI but have NOT been adopted into GB CLP; or (iii) GB uses GB MCL harmonised classifications instead of EU CLH, so the same substance can be classified differently. GRADE P if it identifies two separate regimes but offers no concrete divergence, or gives a divergence that is not actually correct. GRADE I if it treats the UK as a single CLP regime or assumes EU = GB.
expert_notes: Verified current as of June 2026. Core concept (separate, diverging regimes) is high-confidence. UFI/PCN example is valid but carry the nuance that GB treats Annex VIII as voluntary rather than absent; prefer 'voluntary in GB / mandatory in EU+NI' phrasing. The new-hazard-classes divergence (ED/PBT/PMT, not adopted in GB) is the cleanest grading anchor and is fully active for mixtures from 1 May 2026. Note the regime is actively reforming: UK SI 2026/484 removed the separate GB C&L notification-to-HSE obligation from 21 May 2026 (distinct from poison-centre notification; does not affect the examples above). Re-confirm divergences at each release date.

source: DSEAR 2002 (SI 2002/2776) — scope (physical safety from fire/explosion) vs COSHH (health)
question: At a fictional coatings plant, a volatile flammable solvent is both an inhalation hazard to workers and a fire/explosion risk. Which regime primarily governs the FIRE AND EXPLOSION risk arising from the solvent?
choices: The Dangerous Substances and Explosive Atmospheres Regulations (DSEAR)
The Control of Substances Hazardous to Health Regulations (COSHH)
GB CLP
EH40 Workplace Exposure Limits
answer: The Dangerous Substances and Explosive Atmospheres Regulations (DSEAR)

distractor_rationale.The Control of Substances Hazardous to H: COSHH governs health effects of hazardous substances, not the fire/explosion (physical safety) risk.
distractor_rationale.GB CLP: GB CLP governs classification, labelling and packaging of chemicals, not workplace control of fire/explosion risk.
distractor_rationale.EH40 Workplace Exposure Limits: EH40 is the list of workplace exposure limits used with COSHH; it is a health-exposure tool, not a fire-risk control regime.

expert_notes: Core scope-mapping item. The same solvent legitimately also engages COSHH for the inhalation hazard; the question is scoped to the fire/explosion risk.

source: DSEAR 2002 Schedule 2 — hazardous-area zoning (gas/vapour Zones 0/1/2)
question: A DSEAR hazardous-area assessment of a fictional solvent-handling room concludes that a flammable vapour explosive atmosphere is 'likely to occur in normal operation'. The correct zone classification for that area is:
choices: Zone 1
Zone 0
Zone 2
Zone 21
answer: Zone 1

distractor_rationale.Zone 0: Zone 0 is where an explosive gas/vapour atmosphere is present continuously, for long periods, or frequently — more onerous than 'likely in normal operation'.
distractor_rationale.Zone 2: Zone 2 is where an explosive atmosphere is NOT likely in normal operation and, if it occurs, persists only briefly — less frequent than this scenario.
distractor_rationale.Zone 21: Zones 20/21/22 are the dust series; this scenario concerns a vapour, which uses the 0/1/2 series.

expert_notes: Confirm zoning definitions match L138/Schedule 2 wording ('likely to occur in normal operation' = Zone 1).

source: CLP (Amendments to Secondary Legislation) Regs 2015 extending DSEAR scope from 1 June 2015 (gases under pressure; substances corrosive to metals)
question: A fictional plant stores nitrogen at high pressure (a 'gas under pressure' under CLP) and a strongly acidic descaler classified 'corrosive to metals'. Considering DSEAR as it applies in 2026, these substances are:
choices: Within DSEAR scope, because the 2015 amendment extended DSEAR to gases under pressure and substances corrosive to metals
Outside DSEAR scope, because DSEAR covers only flammable and explosive substances
Within DSEAR scope only if they are also flammable
Outside DSEAR scope and governed solely by COSHH
answer: Within DSEAR scope, because the 2015 amendment extended DSEAR to gases under pressure and substances corrosive to metals

distractor_rationale.Outside DSEAR scope, because DSEAR cover: This reflects the pre-2015 position; from 1 June 2015 DSEAR scope was extended to these two further CLP physical-hazard classes.
distractor_rationale.Within DSEAR scope only if they are also: The 2015 classes (gases under pressure; corrosive to metals) are independent physical-hazard classes; flammability is not a precondition.
distractor_rationale.Outside DSEAR scope and governed solely: COSHH addresses health effects; gases under pressure and corrosion-to-metal are physical-safety hazards that fall to DSEAR.

expert_notes: Verified against HSE L138 page and CLP (Amendments) Regs 2015. Good post-2015-scope item; relies on the corrected scope position.

source: DSEAR 2002 reg 6 — elimination/reduction hierarchy (prevent the explosive atmosphere before controlling ignition; mitigate last)
question: A fictional process exposes 200 L of a volatile flammable solvent in an open tray. An engineer proposes, as the FIRST and primary measure, installing explosion-protected (ATEX-rated) electrical equipment in the room. Under DSEAR's reg 6 priority, the most appropriate PRIMARY measure is instead to:
choices: Reduce the quantity in use and enclose/contain the operation to prevent a flammable atmosphere forming
Install ATEX-rated electrical equipment as the first line of defence
Issue operators with flame-retardant personal protective equipment
Fit explosion-relief panels to the room
answer: Reduce the quantity in use and enclose/contain the operation to prevent a flammable atmosphere forming

distractor_rationale.Install ATEX-rated electrical equipment: Controlling ignition sources is lower in DSEAR's priority than reducing the substance and preventing the explosive atmosphere from forming.
distractor_rationale.Issue operators with flame-retardant per: PPE is a last-resort/mitigation measure, not the primary control under DSEAR's hierarchy.
distractor_rationale.Fit explosion-relief panels to the room: Explosion relief is a mitigation measure applied only after prevention measures; it does not stop the atmosphere forming.

expert_notes: Confirm DSEAR reg 6 ordering: reduce quantity / avoid-control releases / prevent explosive atmosphere BEFORE ignition control, with mitigation last. Defensive: tests control priority, not how to create the atmosphere.

source: DSEAR 2002 reg 6 — principal hazard identification + top-of-hierarchy control
question: In a fictional small workshop, an operator decants a volatile flammable solvent from a drum into open buckets on a bench about two metres from a running pedestal grinder that throws sparks. There is no mechanical ventilation and no hazardous-area assessment. Identify the principal safety hazard and state a correct top-of-hierarchy control.
criterion: GRADE C only if the answer (a) identifies the PRINCIPAL hazard as the formation and ignition of a flammable/explosive vapour atmosphere (flammable solvent vapour plus a competent ignition source nearby), AND (b) offers a correct top-of-hierarchy DSEAR control — eliminate/reduce the quantity, substitute a less volatile product, enclose/contain the decanting, or prevent the explosive atmosphere via extraction/ventilation — i.e. a measure that prevents the atmosphere rather than only controlling ignition or relying on PPE. GRADE P if the principal hazard is identified but the only control offered is ignition-source control (e.g. move the grinder) or PPE (wrong tier). GRADE I if the principal fire/explosion hazard is missed (e.g. the answer addresses only inhalation toxicity or slips/trips).
expert_notes: Note the deliberate dual nature (also a COSHH inhalation issue); the rubric scores on the fire/explosion principal hazard. A model that ONLY raises inhalation should grade I for this item's intent.

source: DSEAR + COSHH dual application; prevention-before-mitigation and hierarchy ordering
question: A fictional batch reactor is charged with a solvent that is both highly flammable and acutely toxic by inhalation. A junior engineer proposes managing the operation 'under COSHH with respirators and a fire extinguisher to hand'. Evaluate the regulatory framing and the control approach.
criterion: GRADE C only if the answer (a) recognises that BOTH DSEAR (fire/explosion) AND COSHH (inhalation health hazard) apply to the same operation, and (b) corrects the control approach by prioritising prevention and higher-tier controls (eliminate/reduce quantity, closed transfer/containment, engineering controls/LEV, prevent the explosive atmosphere) before PPE, and treats the extinguisher as mitigation/last resort rather than a primary control. GRADE P if only one regime is identified, OR both regimes are named but the control ordering remains PPE-led/muddled. GRADE I if the regimes are conflated (e.g. 'COSHH covers the fire risk') or PPE/extinguisher is accepted as the primary control.
expert_notes: Tests the most common real-world error (treating a dual hazard under one regime, PPE-first). Defensive: no operational charging detail rewarded.

source: COSHH 2002 scope exclusions — lead governed by Control of Lead at Work Regs, not COSHH
question: A worker at a fictional foundry is regularly exposed to airborne lead fume. Which regime PRIMARILY governs the control of that lead exposure?
choices: The Control of Lead at Work Regulations (lead is outside COSHH scope)
The Control of Substances Hazardous to Health Regulations (COSHH)
DSEAR
EH40 Workplace Exposure Limits alone
answer: The Control of Lead at Work Regulations (lead is outside COSHH scope)

distractor_rationale.The Control of Substances Hazardous to H: Lead is expressly excluded from COSHH and is dealt with under its own dedicated regulations.
distractor_rationale.DSEAR: DSEAR addresses fire/explosion and related physical-safety hazards, not the health effects of lead.
distractor_rationale.EH40 Workplace Exposure Limits alone: EH40 lists exposure limits but is not itself the controlling regime; lead has its own regulatory regime and action levels.

expert_notes: Tests the 'adjacent but wrong regulation' trap. Confirm lead exclusion from COSHH; note the CLAW 2002 amendment consultation is a watch item but does not change the exclusion.

source: COSHH 2002 Schedule 2A — the eight principles of good control practice are NOT ranked (HSE good-control-practice guidance)
question: A site manager asserts that, under the COSHH eight principles of good control practice, the principle on minimising emission must always be fully implemented BEFORE the principle on informing and training employees, because the principles form a ranked sequence. The correct position is:
choices: The eight principles are not ranked; all of them must be applied together to achieve adequate control
Correct — the eight principles are a strict sequence that must be followed in order
Only the first five principles are mandatory; the rest are advisory
The principles apply only to carcinogens and mutagens
answer: The eight principles are not ranked; all of them must be applied together to achieve adequate control

distractor_rationale.Correct — the eight principles are a str: HSE states the principles are not ordered by rank; they must all be applied, not worked through as a strict sequence.
distractor_rationale.Only the first five principles are manda: All eight principles form good control practice; none is downgraded to advisory.
distractor_rationale.The principles apply only to carcinogens: The principles apply to substances hazardous to health generally, not only CMR substances.

expert_notes: Verified against HSE 'Principles of good control practice'. Distinguish the (unranked) eight principles from the (ranked) hierarchy of control.

source: EH40 / COSHH — WEL short-term exposure limit (15-min STEL) vs long-term limit (8-hr TWA)
question: A solvent has both a 15-minute reference-period workplace exposure limit and an 8-hour reference-period limit. A monitored task produces a single exposure spike lasting about 10 minutes. That spike should principally be assessed against:
choices: The short-term exposure limit (STEL), which uses a 15-minute reference period
The long-term exposure limit, which uses an 8-hour time-weighted average
Neither limit, because the spike lasted less than 15 minutes
Both limits combined into a single averaged value
answer: The short-term exposure limit (STEL), which uses a 15-minute reference period

distractor_rationale.The long-term exposure limit, which uses: The 8-hour TWA addresses chronic/averaged exposure over a shift, not a short acute spike.
distractor_rationale.Neither limit, because the spike lasted: A spike shorter than 15 minutes is still assessed against the 15-minute STEL reference period; it is not exempt.
distractor_rationale.Both limits combined into a single avera: The STEL and the 8-hour TWA are distinct limits with different reference periods; they are not merged into one averaged value.

expert_notes: Confirm STEL = 15-minute reference period and the principle that short spikes are assessed against the STEL.

source: COSHH 2002 reg 11 — health surveillance triggers (identifiable disease, reasonable likelihood, valid technique)
question: Workers at a fictional site use a respiratory sensitiser known to cause occupational asthma, and a valid technique exists to detect early health effects. Under COSHH regulation 11, health surveillance is:
choices: Required, because there is exposure to a substance linked to an identifiable disease with a reasonable likelihood of occurrence and a valid detection technique exists
Not required unless a worker has already developed asthma symptoms
Required only once a workplace exposure limit has been exceeded
Discretionary and decided solely by each employee
answer: Required, because there is exposure to a substance linked to an identifiable disease with a reasonable likelihood of occurrence and a valid detection technique exists

distractor_rationale.Not required unless a worker has already: Health surveillance is preventive and triggered by the exposure/likelihood/detectability conditions, not by waiting for disease to appear.
distractor_rationale.Required only once a workplace exposure: The reg 11 trigger is the identifiable-disease/likelihood/valid-technique test, not WEL exceedance.
distractor_rationale.Discretionary and decided solely by each: Health surveillance, where the conditions are met, is an employer duty, not an employee's discretionary choice.

expert_notes: Confirm reg 11 trigger wording. Sensitisers causing occupational asthma are a recognised trigger area; defensive (tests when surveillance is required, no substance detail).

source: COSHH 2002 reg 7 / Schedule 2A — adequate control and PPE as last resort
question: For a fictional manual cleaning task using a corrosive, hazardous-to-health cleaner, a manager's entire control plan is 'issue everyone gloves and goggles'. Critique this plan against COSHH.
criterion: GRADE C only if the answer (a) identifies that PPE is the last resort in COSHH control and that the plan jumps straight to PPE while skipping higher-tier controls, AND (b) proposes at least one higher-tier option (substitute a less hazardous/lower-concentration product, change the method to reduce contact, or apply engineering controls/containment), referencing adequate control / good control practice. GRADE P if it states PPE-only is insufficient but offers no higher-tier alternative. GRADE I if it endorses the gloves-and-goggles plan as adequate control.
expert_notes: Defensive: tests control selection only. Confirm framing aligns with COSHH reg 7 hierarchy and Schedule 2A principle (e) (PPE in combination, as last resort).

source: COSHH 2002 regs 6/7/11 — assess before work; adequate control; review and surveillance
question: A fictional laboratory plans to introduce a new process that will generate an airborne hazardous dust. Outline what COSHH requires the laboratory to do BEFORE the work starts and how 'adequate control' would be judged once controls are in place.
criterion: GRADE C only if the answer covers all three of: (a) a suitable and sufficient COSHH risk assessment carried out BEFORE the work begins; (b) control of exposure via the hierarchy / eight principles, with adequate control meaning any WEL is not exceeded and (for any CMR/asthmagen) exposure reduced as low as reasonably practicable; and (c) ongoing review/checking of controls and, where the reg 11 trigger is met, health surveillance. GRADE P if it covers risk assessment and control but omits the adequate-control criteria OR the review/monitoring element. GRADE I if it omits the requirement to assess before starting, or treats PPE as the primary control.
expert_notes: Defensive: process is fictional and non-specific. Confirm the adequate-control definition (WEL + ALARP for CMR) matches L5.

source: Hierarchy of control (HSE/COSHH/DSEAR) — elimination is the most effective tier
question: A fictional process can be redesigned so that a hazardous substance is never used at all — the hazard is designed out completely. In the hierarchy of control, this measure is:
choices: Elimination — the most effective control
Substitution
An engineering control
An administrative control
answer: Elimination — the most effective control

distractor_rationale.Substitution: Substitution replaces the hazardous substance with a less hazardous alternative; here the substance is removed entirely, which is elimination.
distractor_rationale.An engineering control: Engineering controls manage a hazard that still exists (e.g. enclosure, LEV); designing the hazard out is more effective than controlling it.
distractor_rationale.An administrative control: Administrative controls rely on procedures/behaviour and sit near the bottom of the hierarchy; removing the hazard is the top tier.

expert_notes: Confirm elimination > substitution ordering.

source: Hierarchy of control — administrative vs engineering control classification
question: To reduce exposure in a fictional packing hall, a company introduces a job-rotation rota that limits the time any one worker spends in a high-exposure area. Within the hierarchy of control, this measure is:
choices: An administrative control
An engineering control
Substitution
Personal protective equipment
answer: An administrative control

distractor_rationale.An engineering control: Engineering controls are physical modifications to the workplace/process (e.g. LEV, enclosure); a rota is a procedural measure, not a physical one.
distractor_rationale.Substitution: Substitution replaces a hazard with a less hazardous one; rotating staff changes who is exposed, not the hazard.
distractor_rationale.Personal protective equipment: No protective equipment is involved; scheduling is administrative.

expert_notes: Classic engineering-vs-administrative confusion. Confirm rotation/scheduling is administrative.

source: Hierarchy of control / COSHH — PPE is the last resort, below feasible engineering controls
question: A risk assessment for a fictional bagging line proposes respirators as the SOLE control for a nuisance dust that could instead be captured at source by local exhaust ventilation (LEV). The hierarchy-of-control problem with this proposal is that:
choices: PPE is the last resort; a feasible higher control (engineering — LEV) should be applied first
PPE and engineering controls are interchangeable, so either choice is acceptable
Respirators are an engineering control, so the proposal is already top-of-hierarchy
PPE is preferred because it protects the individual worker directly
answer: PPE is the last resort; a feasible higher control (engineering — LEV) should be applied first

distractor_rationale.PPE and engineering controls are interch: They are ranked, not interchangeable; engineering controls at source are more reliable than PPE.
distractor_rationale.Respirators are an engineering control,: Respirators are PPE (the lowest tier), not an engineering control.
distractor_rationale.PPE is preferred because it protects the: Reliance on PPE is least reliable (depends on fit, use, maintenance) and is the last resort, not the preferred control.

expert_notes: Reinforces PPE-last and the misclassification of respirators as engineering control.

source: Hierarchy of control / COSHH Schedule 2A principle (h) — substitution must not introduce new/greater risk
question: A flammable solvent in a fictional process is substituted with a less flammable one, which later proves to be a respiratory sensitiser. The correct principle to apply is:
choices: Substitution must be checked for whether the alternative introduces new hazards, and the substitution should be re-assessed
Substitution is always a net improvement because it is high in the hierarchy
The new sensitiser hazard can be disregarded because flammability was reduced
PPE alone now suffices because the substitute is 'safer'
answer: Substitution must be checked for whether the alternative introduces new hazards, and the substitution should be re-assessed

distractor_rationale.Substitution is always a net improvement: A measure's position in the hierarchy does not guarantee it lowers overall risk; a substitute can introduce a worse hazard.
distractor_rationale.The new sensitiser hazard can be disrega: Trading one hazard for another is not acceptable; the transferred/new health hazard must itself be controlled.
distractor_rationale.PPE alone now suffices because the subst: This is PPE-first reasoning and ignores that the new sensitiser hazard requires its own control assessment up the hierarchy.

expert_notes: Maps to COSHH Schedule 2A principle (h) (controls must not increase overall risk). Tests judgement, not a single fact.

source: Hierarchy of control — applying the full hierarchy to a real exposure
question: A fictional fabrication shop has a welding-fume exposure problem. The supervisor's plan is 'everyone wears an FFP3 disposable mask'. Re-plan the controls using the hierarchy of control.
criterion: GRADE C only if the answer (a) places the mask proposal at the bottom tier (PPE) and (b) works upward with concrete higher-tier options appropriate to welding fume — e.g. eliminate/substitute (change process or consumable to reduce fume), engineering controls (local exhaust ventilation / on-tool extraction), then administrative measures — and (c) notes PPE should support, not replace, the higher controls. GRADE P if it lists the hierarchy generically without applying it to welding fume, or leaves PPE as co-equal with engineering controls. GRADE I if it accepts the disposable mask as the primary/sufficient solution.
expert_notes: Defensive and practical. Confirm LEV/on-tool extraction is the expected engineering control for welding fume.

source: Hierarchy of control — detecting and correcting an inverted control plan
question: A draft control plan for a fictional hazardous task lists, as its primary measures, warning signage and personal protective equipment, with no engineering controls considered. Identify the ordering errors in this plan and set out the correct approach.
criterion: GRADE C only if the answer (a) identifies that signage (administrative) and PPE are the lowest tiers being used as primary controls, (b) reorders to elimination/substitution -> engineering controls -> administrative controls -> PPE, and (c) justifies why higher tiers are more reliable (they reduce the hazard at source and do not depend on human behaviour). GRADE P if it reorders correctly but gives no justification, or misplaces one tier. GRADE I if it does not recognise that the plan is inverted.
expert_notes: Tests both recall (the ordering) and reasoning (why). Confirm justification (reliability/independence from behaviour) is the expected rationale.

source: Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 s.2 — primary duty on the employer
question: Under section 2 of the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974, the duty to ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, the health, safety and welfare at work of employees rests primarily on:
choices: The employer
Each individual employee
The HSE inspector
The manufacturer of the work equipment
answer: The employer

distractor_rationale.Each individual employee: Employees have their own duties (e.g. to take reasonable care, under s.7), but the s.2 general duty is the employer's.
distractor_rationale.The HSE inspector: HSE enforces the law; it does not hold the employer's statutory duty of care.
distractor_rationale.The manufacturer of the work equipment: Manufacturers/suppliers have duties under s.6 for articles and substances; that is a different duty from the s.2 employer duty.

expert_notes: Confirm s.2 = employer duty to employees; s.7 = employee duty; s.6 = articles/substances.

source: Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 s.3 — duty to non-employees
question: A fictional firm's activities create risks affecting a contractor's employee and a passing member of the public. The provision that places a duty on the firm to protect those NON-EMPLOYEES is:
choices: Section 3 of the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974
Section 2 of the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974
Section 6 of the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974
Regulation 3 of the Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999
answer: Section 3 of the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974

distractor_rationale.Section 2 of the Health and Safety at Wo: Section 2 concerns the employer's own employees, not non-employees.
distractor_rationale.Section 6 of the Health and Safety at Wo: Section 6 concerns designers/manufacturers/suppliers of articles and substances, not the general duty to non-employees.
distractor_rationale.Regulation 3 of the Management of Health: Reg 3 is the duty to carry out a risk assessment; it is not the general duty protecting non-employees (though it requires assessing risks to them).

expert_notes: Confirm s.3 = non-employees. The MHSWR reg 3 distractor is deliberately close (reg 3 does require assessing risks to non-employees, but is not the general protective duty).

source: ALARP / 'so far as is reasonably practicable' (Edwards v National Coal Board [1949])
question: An engineer argues that a particular control 'must be implemented whatever the cost'. Under the 'so far as is reasonably practicable' / ALARP test as understood in GB, the correct position is:
choices: A measure may be omitted only if its cost or effort would be grossly disproportionate to the risk reduction it achieves
Every technically possible measure must be implemented regardless of cost
Cost and risk are weighed equally, with no bias either way
Controls are required only after an incident has already occurred
answer: A measure may be omitted only if its cost or effort would be grossly disproportionate to the risk reduction it achieves

distractor_rationale.Every technically possible measure must: That describes 'physically possible', not 'reasonably practicable'; the test allows omission where there is gross disproportion.
distractor_rationale.Cost and risk are weighed equally, with: The test is weighted toward safety — a measure is required unless its sacrifice is grossly disproportionate, not merely greater, than the risk.
distractor_rationale.Controls are required only after an inci: The duty is anticipatory; it does not wait for harm to happen.

expert_notes: Confirm the 'gross disproportion' phrasing from Edwards v NCB and the weighting toward safety.

source: Management of Health and Safety at Work Regs 1999 reg 3 — suitable and sufficient assessment; recording at 5+ employees
question: A firm with seven employees carries out a suitable and sufficient risk assessment. Under regulation 3 of the Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999, the firm:
choices: Must record the significant findings of the assessment, because it has five or more employees
Need never record anything, only carry out the assessment
Must record the findings only if it has fifty or more employees
Need not make the assessment suitable and sufficient as long as findings are recorded
answer: Must record the significant findings of the assessment, because it has five or more employees

distractor_rationale.Need never record anything, only carry o: Recording of significant findings is required once an employer has five or more employees.
distractor_rationale.Must record the findings only if it has: The recording threshold is five employees, not fifty.
distractor_rationale.Need not make the assessment suitable an: The assessment must be suitable and sufficient regardless of recording; recording does not substitute for adequacy.

expert_notes: Confirm reg 3 recording threshold (5+ employees) and the suitable-and-sufficient standard.

source: MHSWR 1999 reg 3 + HSWA s.2 — risk assessment is anticipatory, not contingent on past incidents
question: A manager says: 'We don't need a written risk assessment for this task — we've done it for years and never had an accident.' Evaluate this reasoning against the GB legal framework.
criterion: GRADE C only if the answer (a) explains that the duty to assess risk is anticipatory and does not depend on whether harm has occurred, (b) references the MHSWR reg 3 duty to carry out a suitable and sufficient assessment (and to record significant findings where five or more employees), and (c) ties this to the HSWA general duty to control risk so far as is reasonably practicable. GRADE P if it concludes an assessment is needed but without the anticipatory rationale or the legal grounding. GRADE I if it accepts the 'no accidents, so no assessment needed' reasoning.
expert_notes: Confirm the recording threshold reference is correct (5+). Tests application of the legal duty to a common excuse.

source: ALARP / reasonably practicable applied to facts (Edwards v National Coal Board)
question: At a fictional plant, a feasible and modestly priced machine guard is deferred on a hazardous machine because 'the risk is fairly low and budgets are tight'. Apply the 'reasonably practicable' test to this decision and reach a defensible conclusion.
criterion: GRADE C only if the answer (a) applies the Edwards v NCB balance — weighing the quantum of risk against the sacrifice in money, time or trouble — and explains that a measure may be omitted only where the sacrifice is grossly disproportionate to the risk reduction, weighted toward safety, AND (b) reaches a defensible conclusion that a modest cost set against a real (even if not high) risk is unlikely to be grossly disproportionate, so the guard is likely required. GRADE P if it recites the ALARP/reasonably-practicable test but does not apply it to these facts, or reaches a conclusion it does not justify. GRADE I if it treats the decision as a straight cost-benefit choice or asserts that low risk alone excuses the measure.
expert_notes: Confirm the 'gross disproportion' standard and that budget tightness alone is not a defence. Reasonable experts could vary on the final call given vague figures — rubric rewards correct application, not a fixed verdict.

source: HAZOP — purpose of guidewords (systematic prompts for deviations from design intent)
question: During a HAZOP of a fictional process, the team applies the guideword 'MORE' to the parameter 'pressure' at a particular node. The purpose of doing this is to:
choices: Systematically prompt the team to consider a deviation from design intent — here, higher-than-intended pressure
Calculate the set pressure of the relief valve
Assign a numerical risk score to the node
Confirm that the process is operating exactly as designed
answer: Systematically prompt the team to consider a deviation from design intent — here, higher-than-intended pressure

distractor_rationale.Calculate the set pressure of the relief: Relief sizing is a separate design/engineering calculation, not the function of a HAZOP guideword.
distractor_rationale.Assign a numerical risk score to the nod: HAZOP is a qualitative hazard-identification technique; numerical risk ranking is not the guideword's role (and is closer to LOPA/QRA).
distractor_rationale.Confirm that the process is operating ex: Guidewords are used to probe DEVIATIONS from design intent, not to confirm normal operation.

expert_notes: Concept-level only; defensive. Confirm guideword/parameter framing matches standard HAZOP description.

source: LOPA — Independent Protection Layer (IPL) criteria: independence from the initiating cause
question: In a LOPA for a fictional system, a proposed safeguard relies on the SAME sensor as the basic process control loop whose failure is the initiating event. As an Independent Protection Layer (IPL), this safeguard is:
choices: Not valid, because an IPL must be independent of the initiating cause and of other protection layers
Valid, because it is automated
Valid, because it is auditable
Valid, because basic process control always counts as an IPL
answer: Not valid, because an IPL must be independent of the initiating cause and of other protection layers

distractor_rationale.Valid, because it is automated: Automation does not satisfy the independence requirement; sharing the sensor with the initiating cause is a common-cause failure.
distractor_rationale.Valid, because it is auditable: Auditability is necessary but not sufficient; the layer still fails the independence criterion.
distractor_rationale.Valid, because basic process control alw: A control function that can cause the initiating event cannot simultaneously be claimed as an independent layer protecting against it.

expert_notes: Confirm IPL criteria (effective, independent, auditable). Concept-level; defensive.

source: LOPA — prevention vs mitigation layers (secondary containment / bund is mitigation)
question: A bund (secondary containment) is constructed around a fictional storage tank. In layers-of-protection terms, the bund is best described as:
choices: A mitigation layer, reducing the consequence after a loss of containment
A prevention layer, stopping the loss of containment from occurring
An initiating cause of the scenario
A basic process control function
answer: A mitigation layer, reducing the consequence after a loss of containment

distractor_rationale.A prevention layer, stopping the loss of: A bund acts only after a release has occurred (it contains the spill); it does not prevent the loss of containment.
distractor_rationale.An initiating cause of the scenario: A bund is a safeguard, not a cause of the hazardous event.
distractor_rationale.A basic process control function: A bund is passive physical containment, not a process control loop.

expert_notes: Confirm prevention-vs-mitigation classification of secondary containment. Concept-level; defensive.

source: HAZOP vs LOPA roles — selecting the right method to test safeguard adequacy against a target
question: A study team has used HAZOP to identify a high-consequence deviation and now wants to test whether the existing safeguards reduce its frequency to a tolerable risk target. The most appropriate next method is:
choices: LOPA — a semi-quantitative analysis of independent protection layers against a risk target
A repeat HAZOP using additional guidewords
A personal protective equipment assessment
No further analysis, because the HAZOP already listed the safeguards
answer: LOPA — a semi-quantitative analysis of independent protection layers against a risk target

distractor_rationale.A repeat HAZOP using additional guidewor: HAZOP identifies deviations and lists safeguards but does not quantify whether they reduce risk to a target.
distractor_rationale.A personal protective equipment assessme: Wrong level of analysis — this is a process-risk adequacy question, not a personal-protection question.
distractor_rationale.No further analysis, because the HAZOP a: Listing safeguards is not the same as testing whether they are adequate against a tolerable-risk target.

expert_notes: Confirm HAZOP (identify) vs LOPA (test adequacy/quantify) role distinction. Concept-level; defensive.

source: LOPA / IPL — adequacy of a single alarm-plus-operator layer
question: A study team reviewing a fictional exothermic batch reactor lists a single safeguard — a high-temperature alarm with operator response — and concludes the risk is adequately controlled. Critique this conclusion using layers-of-protection and IPL concepts. (Do not provide any operational or design parameters.)
criterion: GRADE C only if the answer (a) questions whether one alarm-plus-operator layer is sufficient for a high-consequence event, (b) applies the IPL criteria (effective, independent, auditable) and recognises that an operator-response layer has limited and variable reliability, and (c) suggests that additional independent layers may be needed (e.g. an independent high-integrity trip/safety instrumented function) WITHOUT giving operational or design detail. GRADE P if it raises a concern about adequacy but does not use IPL criteria or reliability reasoning. GRADE I if it accepts the single alarm-plus-operator layer as adequate. AUTO-FAIL any answer that supplies operational setpoints, runaway-reaction procedures, or other detail outside the defensive scope.
expert_notes: Defensive guardrail built into the rubric (auto-fail on operational detail). Confirm the IPL reliability point is the intended C-grade content.

source: LOPA / IPL — independence and common-cause failure
question: Two safeguards are presented as 'two independent protection layers', but on inspection both rely on the same sensor and the same power supply. Reason about whether these count as two independent layers and why the answer matters for the risk-reduction claim.
criterion: GRADE C only if the answer (a) identifies that the shared sensor and shared power supply create a common-cause failure that defeats independence, (b) concludes the two safeguards effectively function as ONE protection layer, not two, and (c) explains why this matters — the claimed risk reduction is overstated because a single fault can disable both. GRADE P if it notes a problem but does not name common-cause failure / the independence criterion. GRADE I if it accepts both as valid independent layers.
expert_notes: Confirm common-cause failure is the intended core concept. Concept-level; defensive.
